name: Bytecode-to-Source Side Tables
summary: Side tables mapping bytecode offsets back to source positions for stack traces, debuggers, and exception messages. JVM `LineNumberTable`, CPython PEP 657 columns, Lua line tables, LuaJIT adaptive width. The compact-encoding cousin to DWARF .debug_line.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Most bytecode formats need to map bytecode offsets back to source positions for stack traces, debugger stepping, and runtime error messages. Storing per-instruction source positions naively wastes substantial space — a 4-byte position per 1–4 byte instruction more than doubles the bytecode footprint. The recurring solution is a side table: a compact encoding of `(bytecode_offset, source_position)` pairs, sometimes with delta encoding or run-length compression.

This record covers bytecode-side tables. The compact native-code analogue is DWARF `.debug_line`; the JS-source-map analogue is covered separately.

sections.title: JVM LineNumberTable and LocalVariableTable
sections.content:
JVM `LineNumberTable` is an array of `(bytecode_pc: u16, line_number: u16)` pairs per method, 4 bytes per entry, line-only granularity. Generated by default by `javac` even without explicit `-g`, but omittable with `-g:none` and compiler-dependent for other JVM-language compilers (Kotlin, Scala, Clojure all generate their own variants).

`LocalVariableTable` is separate: 10 bytes per variable entry encoding `(start_pc, length, name_index, descriptor_index, slot)`. The line-only granularity of `LineNumberTable` is a deliberate trade-off — sufficient for stack traces and debugger stepping, and keeps class files compact.

sections.title: CPython PEP 657 — column ranges in tracebacks
sections.content:
Python 3.11 (PEP 657, 2022) extended CPython's source-position metadata to include `(start_line, end_line, start_col, end_col)` per instruction. Column offsets are stored as `uint8_t` (range 0–255). The change made tracebacks point to the exact subexpression that failed (the famous `^^^` underline), at the cost of `.pyc` size — location tables grew about 9× over Python 3.10, overall `.pyc` size increased about 22%.

The size cost is opt-out via `PYTHONNODEBUGRANGES` or `-Xno_debug_ranges`. The motivation was developer experience: knowing that an error happened at *which subexpression* of a long expression line is dramatically more useful than line-only positions, especially for f-strings, chained method calls, and complex literals.

sections.title: Lua and LuaJIT — adaptive width encoding
sections.content:
Standard Lua 5.4 stores 4 bytes per instruction for line info. The NodeMCU **Lua Compact Debug** variant replaces this with run-length encoding of line deltas, dramatically reducing footprint for embedded targets where every kilobyte counts.

LuaJIT uses adaptive width encoding: `u8` for ≤255 lines, `u16` for ≤65535, `u32` otherwise. The adaptive approach is elegant — most functions are short, so most line numbers fit in a byte; the wider encodings activate only for functions large enough that the overhead doesn't matter. This is a recurring pattern: pick the column type to match the column's actual value distribution.

sections.title: Design principle — exploit regularity
sections.content:
Most compact encodings here exploit the same observation: consecutive bytecode instructions usually correspond to consecutive or nearby source positions. JVM and CPython are simple side-table baselines; Lua Compact Debug, LuaJIT, DWARF, and source maps more aggressively exploit regularity through compression.

This regularity is captured by run-length encoding (Lua Compact Debug), adaptive width encoding (LuaJIT), state-machine special opcodes (DWARF), VLQ delta encoding (source maps), or bounded-width column offsets (PEP 657). Picking one of these families early matters because the dominant cost is *read* performance during stack-trace formatting and exception throwing — a format that is cheap to decode sequentially but occasionally random-accessed by binary search is the practical sweet spot.
